title: Higher-order interactions in ecology can be hidden in plain sight
authors: Violeta Calleja-Solanas, Santiago Lamata-Otín, Carlos Gómez-Ambrosi, Jesús Gómez-Gardeñes, Sandro Meloni
abstract: Higher-order interactions are increasingly recognized as a key component of ecological dynamics. However, we show that higher-order Lotka-Volterra dynamics can, in some scenarios, be accurately reproduced by effective pairwise models fitted to the same abundance time series. Consequently, higher-order interactions cannot, in general, be inferred from time-series data alone. We further identify a fundamental problem of mechanistic identifiability, whereby distinct interaction mechanisms generate nearly indistinguishable dynamics, potentially leading to accurate yet misleading ecological interpretations. Our results highlight the need to complement time-series data with additional ecological information to infer interaction structure reliably.
summary: This paper shows that higher-order interactions in complex systems can be 'hidden' because simpler pairwise models can accurately predict population dynamics while providing a false description of the underlying interaction structure. It identifies a fundamental limit to inferring complex interaction networks from time-series data alone.
main_problem: The paper investigates the 'mechanistic identifiability' problem: whether higher-order interactions (HOIs) in ecological communities can be uniquely detected from species abundance time-series data or if they can be hidden by effective pairwise models.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that higher-order Lotka-Volterra dynamics can be accurately mimicked by pairwise models, leading to high predictive accuracy but fundamentally incorrect ecological interpretations (misidentifying species roles).
method: The study uses a computational pipeline involving numerical integration of higher-order Lotka-Volterra systems, linear least-squares regression to fit growth rates to pairwise hyperplanes, and the use of Hessian matrices to detect curvature in growth rate functions.
model_or_system: The system consists of multi-species ecological communities modeled using higher-order Lotka-Volterra (hoLV) equations, which include intrinsic growth rates, pairwise interactions, and triadic (three-species) interactions.
key_observables: Species abundance trajectories, per capita growth rates, system attractors (equilibrium vs. limit cycles), and the Hessian matrix of growth rates.
important_parameters: Intrinsic growth rates (ri), pairwise interaction coefficients (aij), higher-order interaction coefficients (bijk), and the relative weight of HOIs (theta).
main_assumptions: The analysis uses idealized in silico conditions with high-resolution trajectories and no observation error to test fundamental limits of identifiability.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the methodology flowchart; Figure 2 compares detectable vs. undetectable systems using 3-species models; Figure 3 shows a 7-species multi-trophic system where pairwise approximations remain accurate despite HOIs; Figure 4 illustrates how Jacobian entries can fail to show sign changes even when HOIs are present.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of mechanistic identifiability, defines the higher-order Lotka-Volterra model, presents a computational pipeline for inference, categorizes failure modes of detection (detectable vs. hidden regimes), proposes the Hessian matrix as a diagnostic tool, and demonstrates the scalability of the phenomenon in complex communities.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Workflow to assess whether higher-order interac- tions are detectable from abundance time series. From the time series of abundances obtained by integrating the orig- inal higher-order system, we evaluate the per capita growth rates, fit them to a hyperplane to infer interaction coefficients and intrinsic growth rates, and integrate the resulting pairwise system. The final comparison between the inferred and orig- inal dynamics reveals whether higher-order interactions are detectable.

FIG. 2: Examples of detectable and undetectable higher-order interactions. Panels a–d correspond to System 1 (detectable), and panels e–h to System 2 (undetectable). Panels a and e show the original interaction structure, including higher-order interactions (join arrows), while panels b and f display the inferred pairwise interaction networks obtained from the fitting procedure. In these network representations, green (red) arrows indicate positive (negative) interaction coefficients. Panels c and g compare the original and the inferred attractors, and panels d and h compare the corresponding time series of species 3. In System 1, higher-order interactions leave dynamical signatures that...

FIG. 3: Undetectable higher-order interactions in a multispecies system. Panels a and b show the original pairwise and higher-order interaction structure. The inferred pairwise interaction network (panels c and d) present two misidentified interac- tions (between species 2 and 4, and species 3 and 6), as well as new spurious interactions. Green (red) arrows indicate positive (negative) interaction coefficients. Panel e shows the comparison between the original and inferred time series of species abun- dances.
